apprentice.questionText: What is the ampere rating for a mobile home power-supply cord attachment plug cap?
apprentice.explanation: Per NEC 550.10(C), the attachment plug cap for a mobile home power-supply cord must be a 125/250-volt, 3-pole, 4-wire, grounding type rated 50 amperes.
apprentice.sparkyTip: Mobile home plugs are always 50A — same size as a range outlet. If you see a 4-prong 50A plug, think mobile home or electric range!

apprentice.questionText: The secondary electrical distribution system for a mobile home park must be what type?
apprentice.explanation: Per NEC 550.30, the secondary electrical distribution system for mobile home parks to mobile home lots must be single-phase, 120/240-volt, nominal.
apprentice.sparkyTip: Mobile homes are residential — they always get single-phase 120/240V, just like a regular house!

apprentice.questionText: The mobile home power-supply cord attachment plug cap must be what configuration?
apprentice.explanation: Per NEC 550.10(C), the plug cap must be a 3-pole, 4-wire, grounding type. The 4 wires consist of 2 ungrounded (hot) conductors, 1 grounded (neutral) conductor, and 1 equipment grounding conductor.
apprentice.sparkyTip: 3 poles = 2 hots + 1 neutral. The 4th wire is the equipment ground — that's what makes it a 'grounding type' connector.

apprentice.questionText: All 125-volt receptacles that supply power to a pool pump motor must have what type of protection?
apprentice.explanation: NEC 680.22(A) requires that all 125-volt, single-phase receptacles rated 30 amperes or less and located within 20 feet of the inside walls of a pool shall be protected by a ground-fault circuit interrupter (GFCI). This includes receptacles supplying pool pump motors.
apprentice.sparkyTip: GFCI is the golden rule near water. Any 125V receptacle within 20 feet of the pool edge needs it — no exceptions for pool pump receptacles!

apprentice.questionText: What is the minimum distance a receptacle outlet must be located from the inside wall of a pool?
apprentice.explanation: NEC 680.22(A)(5) requires receptacles to be located not less than 6 feet from the inside walls of the pool. At least one 125-volt receptacle must be located between 6 and 20 feet from the pool.
apprentice.sparkyTip: Think of it as the "6-20 rule" — receptacles must be at least 6 feet away, but at least one must be within 20 feet so pool equipment can be plugged in.

apprentice.questionText: Which of the following must be included in the equipotential bonding grid for a permanently installed pool per NEC 680.26(B)?
options: Metal parts of the pool structure, metal fittings, and forming shells
Only the pool pump motor frame
apprentice.explanation: NEC 680.26(B) requires bonding of all metal parts of the pool structure, including the reinforcing steel, metal fittings within or attached to the pool, forming shells, mounting brackets, and metal parts of the pool cover mechanism. This equipotential bonding grid reduces voltage gradients in the pool area.
apprentice.sparkyTip: Think "bond everything metal" near the pool. The equipotential bonding grid ties all metal together so there is no voltage difference that could shock a swimmer.

apprentice.questionText: What is the minimum burial depth for underground wiring that is not beneath a building, slab, or paving and supplies a pool, spa, or hot tub?
apprentice.explanation: NEC 680.10 specifies that underground wiring shall not be installed under the pool or within 5 feet horizontally of the pool unless necessary to supply pool equipment. The burial depth must comply with Table 300.5, which generally requires 18 inches minimum for direct-burial cables.
apprentice.sparkyTip: Pool underground wiring follows Table 300.5 for burial depth. For most residential pool installations with direct-burial cable, that means 18 inches minimum.

apprentice.questionText: Are extension cords permitted to be used with pool pumps and other pool equipment?
options: Yes, as long as they are outdoor-rated
Yes, if they have GFCI protection
No, extension cords are not permitted for pool equipment
Yes, but only if the cord is 25 feet or shorter
apprentice.explanation: NEC 680.7 prohibits the use of flexible cords and extension cords for pool pump motors and other fixed pool equipment. All pool equipment must be hard-wired or connected via a suitable cord-and-plug connection as permitted for specific equipment.
apprentice.sparkyTip: Extension cords + pools = never allowed. This is a safety no-brainer. Pool equipment gets a permanent wiring connection, period.

apprentice.questionText: Which of the following is NOT a correct classification of hazardous materials?
options: Class I locations are hazardous because of flammable gases or vapors
Class II locations are hazardous because of combustible dusts
Class III locations are hazardous because of easily ignitable fibers or flyings
Class IV locations are hazardous because of pyrophoric liquids or corrosive aerosols

explanations.default: The NEC strictly categorizes hazardous locations into three classes: Class I (Gases/Vapors), Class II (Dusts), and Class III (Fibers/Flyings) per NEC 500.5(B), (C), and (D). There is no 'Class IV' designation in the National Electrical Code.

apprentice.sparkyTip: Field Logic Tip 💡: Just remember 1-2-3 = Gas-Dust-Fiber! The NEC stops at Class III. If you see 'Class IV' on an exam, it is always a trick answer.
apprentice.explanation: Per 500.5(A), hazardous locations are classified only as Class I (flammable gases/vapors/liquids), Class II (combustible dust), or Class III (ignitable fibers/flyings). There is no Class IV in the NEC — pyrophoric liquids and corrosive aerosols are addressed elsewhere in the code but are not given a 'Class IV' designation.

apprentice.questionText: Which of the following statements regarding Hazardous Location Divisions is NOT correct?
options: Division 1 means the hazard is present in the air during normal, everyday operations.
Division 2 means the hazard is only present during an accident, like a spill or broken pipe.
Division 2 locations are considered more dangerous and require stricter wiring methods than Division 1.
A paint booth where flammable vapors are constantly present while painting is classified as Division 1.

explanations.default: Division 1 locations are the most hazardous because the explosive material is expected to be present during normal operations. Therefore, Division 1 requires the strictest wiring methods, such as poured conduit seals and heavy explosionproof enclosures. Division 2 locations have less restrictive wiring requirements because the hazard is normally contained and only present under abnormal conditions.

apprentice.sparkyTip: Field Logic Tip 💡: Number 1 is always the big boss! Division 1 means the danger is expected to be there, so it has the heaviest, strictest, and most expensive wiring rules. Division 2 means the danger is normally locked up safely in a drum or pipe.
apprentice.explanation: Per 500.5(B), Division 1 is the more hazardous designation — flammable concentrations are present under normal operations. Division 2 covers conditions where the hazard is only present under abnormal/accidental conditions (broken containers, equipment failure). Division 1 demands stricter wiring methods (explosionproof, intrinsically safe), not Division 2.

apprentice.questionText: Which term does the NEC use to categorize the specific chemical or dust involved in a hazardous location?

explanations.default: While 'Class' identifies the physical state and 'Division' identifies the likelihood, 'Group' (A through G) classifies the specific combustible material or chemical.

apprentice.sparkyTip: Field Logic Tip 💡: Class = State (Gas/Dust). Division = Situation (Everyday/Accident). Group = The exact chemical!
apprentice.explanation: Per 500.6, the NEC uses 'Group' to categorize the specific gas, vapor, or dust within a hazardous location (e.g., Group A acetylene, Group D gasoline, Group E combustible metal dust). Class identifies the state of the hazard; Division identifies the situation; Group identifies the specific material.

journeyman.questionText: What is the minimum calculated load per mobile home lot used for park service and feeder calculations?
journeyman.explanation: Per NEC 550.31, park electrical wiring systems shall be calculated on the basis of not less than 16,000 volt-amperes for each mobile home lot.
journeyman.sparkyTip: 16,000 VA per lot is the baseline for every mobile home park calculation. Even if the actual home uses less, you calculate with 16,000 VA minimum.

journeyman.questionText: What is the maximum permitted length for a mobile home power-supply cord?
journeyman.explanation: Per NEC 550.10(D), the power-supply cord must not exceed 36½ feet (11.0 m) in length, measured from the attachment plug cap to the point of entrance to the mobile home.
journeyman.sparkyTip: Max cord = 36½ feet, min = 21 feet. The cord needs to reach the pedestal but not be so long it creates a hazard.

journeyman.questionText: In a mobile home, the grounded conductor and the equipment grounding conductor must be:
options: Bonded together at the mobile home panelboard
Connected at the power-supply cord plug
Kept separate throughout the mobile home
Bonded at any accessible point in the home
journeyman.explanation: Per NEC 550.16(A), the grounded (neutral) conductor and the equipment grounding conductor must be kept separate in a mobile home. The neutral-to-ground bond is made at the service disconnecting means (the outdoor pedestal), not inside the mobile home.
journeyman.sparkyTip: A mobile home panelboard works like a sub-panel — neutral and ground stay separate! The bond happens at the outdoor disconnect.

journeyman.questionText: Where must the mobile home service equipment be located?
options: Inside the mobile home, adjacent to the panelboard
In a readily accessible outdoor location within sight of the home
At the property line of the mobile home lot
journeyman.explanation: Per NEC 550.32(A), mobile home service equipment must not be mounted in or on the mobile home. It must be located in a readily accessible outdoor location and within sight from the mobile home it serves.
journeyman.sparkyTip: Service equipment goes OUTSIDE and within sight — think of the familiar power pedestal standing next to the home. Never inside or under the unit!

journeyman.questionText: What is the minimum permitted length for a mobile home power-supply cord?
journeyman.explanation: Per NEC 550.10(D), the power-supply cord must be not less than 21 feet (6.4 m) in length, measured from the attachment plug cap to the point of entrance to the mobile home.
journeyman.sparkyTip: Min 21 feet, max 36½ feet. The minimum ensures you can reach the disconnect pedestal from most lot configurations.

journeyman.questionText: The mobile home power-supply cord must be permanently connected to which component?
options: An exterior-mounted junction box on the chassis
A receptacle outlet on the mobile home frame
The distribution panelboard or a junction box inside the mobile home
The main service disconnect at the lot pedestal
journeyman.explanation: Per NEC 550.10(B), the power-supply cord must be permanently connected to the distribution panelboard or to a junction box permanently connected to the panelboard, with both located inside the mobile home.
journeyman.sparkyTip: The cord connects inside to the panel — it is a permanent connection, not a plug-in. The only plug is at the other end, at the lot pedestal.

journeyman.questionText: Per NEC 680.8, what is the minimum clearance for overhead conductors (insulated supply or service drop cables at 0-750V to ground) above the pool water level or diving platform?
journeyman.explanation: NEC 680.8 Table requires that insulated supply or service drop cables (0-750V to ground) maintain a minimum clearance of 22.5 feet above the maximum water level of the pool, the diving structure, or the observation stand/tower/platform.
journeyman.sparkyTip: The 22.5-foot clearance above pool water applies to insulated service drop cables. All other overhead conductors (not voltage-limited) require even more — 25 feet.

journeyman.questionText: What is the minimum size of the solid copper bonding conductor used in the equipotential bonding grid for a pool?
journeyman.explanation: NEC 680.26(B)(1) requires that the equipotential bonding grid use a minimum 8 AWG solid copper conductor. This conductor connects all metal parts and creates a grid that equalizes the voltage potential around the pool.
journeyman.sparkyTip: Remember: #8 solid copper for pool bonding grid. It must be SOLID, not stranded — solid copper holds up better when buried in concrete or soil.

journeyman.questionText: Per NEC 680.23(A)(3), what protection is required for all luminaires installed in the walls of a pool that operate at more than the low voltage contact limit?
journeyman.explanation: NEC 680.23(A)(3) requires that a GFCI shall protect all luminaires operating at more than the low voltage contact limit that are installed in the walls of pools. This provides a critical layer of safety for underwater lighting.
journeyman.sparkyTip: Underwater lights above low voltage contact limit (15V) MUST have GFCI protection. This is non-negotiable — electricity and water right next to swimmers.

journeyman.questionText: Per NEC 680.23(A)(5), what is the maximum voltage permitted for luminaires installed in a pool?
journeyman.explanation: NEC 680.23(A)(5) limits luminaires installed in pools to a maximum of 150 volts between conductors. This voltage limitation helps reduce the risk of electric shock to swimmers in the event of a luminaire failure.
journeyman.sparkyTip: 150 volts max for pool lights. Most pool luminaires actually operate at 12V via a transformer, but the NEC caps it at 150V as an absolute maximum.

journeyman.questionText: Which wiring method is required within 5 feet of the inside wall of a pool for branch circuits that supply pool equipment?
options: Rigid metal conduit, IMC, or a listed nonmetallic raceway system
journeyman.explanation: NEC 680.21(A) requires that wiring methods within 5 feet of the inside wall of the pool use rigid metal conduit (RMC), intermediate metal conduit (IMC), liquidtight flexible nonmetallic conduit, or a rigid nonmetallic conduit system. NM cable and MC cable are generally not permitted within this zone.
journeyman.sparkyTip: Within 5 feet of the pool wall, think rigid raceways only. No Romex, no standard MC cable. The pool zone demands heavy-duty wiring protection.

journeyman.questionText: In a hospital's general care (Category 2) space, a patient bed location must be supplied by which two branch circuits?
options: One from the equipment branch and one from the normal system; normal circuits can originate from multiple panelboards
One from the life safety branch and one from the normal system; normal circuits must originate in the same panelboard
One from the critical branch and one from the normal system; all normal branch circuits must originate in the same panelboard
Two from the critical branch; originating from separate transfer switches
journeyman.explanation: Per Section 517.18(A), each patient bed location in a general care area requires at least two branch circuits: one from the critical branch and one from the normal system. Additionally, to ensure safe and predictable maintenance, all branch circuits from the normal system serving that specific bed location must originate in the exact same panelboard.
journeyman.sparkyTip: Sparky says: Keep it 'Normal' and 'Critical'! Remember, while you must have two distinct power sources, all your normal power circuits have to live in the exact same panelboard so a technician can quickly isolate them if needed.

journeyman.questionText: In a hospital facility, which branch of the essential electrical system must supply the power for exit signs and the illumination of the means of egress?
journeyman.explanation: Exit signs and egress lighting are strictly for the safe evacuation of a building during an emergency. Therefore, they must be supplied by the Life Safety Branch of the essential electrical system. A common mistake is selecting the Critical Branch, which is instead dedicated to patient care areas and task lighting.
journeyman.sparkyTip: Sparky says: If it helps people get OUT alive, it belongs on Life Safety! Don't confuse it with the Critical Branch, which is for keeping patients ALIVE in their beds.

journeyman.questionText: An electrician is wiring a storage room where sealed 55-gallon drums of flammable solvents are kept. Under normal conditions, no vapors are present in the air. The hazardous vapors would only escape in the event of an accidental spill or a drum rupture. How should this location be classified according to the NEC?

explanations.default: Per NEC 500.5(B)(2), a Class I, Division 2 location includes areas where volatile flammable liquids or gases are handled, processed, or used, but in which they will normally be confined within closed containers or systems from which they can escape only in case of accidental rupture or breakdown.

journeyman.sparkyTip: Field Logic Tip 💡: Apply the rule of thumb! Division 1 = Everyday operations. Division 2 = Accidental conditions. Since the flammable vapors are safely sealed in drums and only escape if a drum breaks (an accident), it is a Division 2 location.
journeyman.explanation: Per 500.5(B)(2), a Class I, Division 2 location is one where flammable liquids/vapors are normally confined in closed containers and would only be released by accidental rupture or failure. Sealed drums of solvent that release vapor only on accidental spill fit this definition exactly. It is Class I (flammable liquid), Division 2 (accidental condition only).

journeyman.questionText: Once an area is defined by its Class and Division, the NEC further categorizes the specific hazardous materials into "Groups" based on their explosive properties. Which of the following materials is categorized as a Class I, Group A hazard—the most volatile gas designation in the code?

explanations.default: NEC 500.6 categorizes materials into specific Groups based on their ignition temperatures and explosion pressures. Group A represents atmospheres containing acetylene, which requires the most stringent explosionproof equipment design due to its extreme volatility. (For context: Hydrogen is Group B, and common fuels like Gasoline and Propane fall under Group D).

journeyman.sparkyTip: Field Logic Tip 💡: The Class tells you the physical state (gas/dust), the Division tells you how often it's there, and the Group categorizes the exact chemical family. Just remember 'A is for Acetylene'—it is the top of the food chain for explosive gases!
journeyman.explanation: Per 500.6(A)(1), Class I Group A is reserved specifically for acetylene because it has the lowest minimum ignition energy and the most extreme explosive characteristics of all Class I gases. Hydrogen is Group B, gasoline/propane fall in Group D, and combustible metal dust is a Class II hazard (Group E), not Class I.

journeyman.questionText: In the NEC, hazardous locations are defined by their Class (gas, dust, or fiber) and Division (normal or accidental). What term is used to further categorize the exact type of chemical or material involved, such as distinguishing hydrogen from gasoline?

explanations.default: While the 'Class' identifies the physical state of the hazard and the 'Division' identifies the likelihood of it being present, NEC 500.6 uses 'Groups' (designated by letters A through G) to classify the specific combustible material, chemical, or dust involved.

journeyman.sparkyTip: Field Logic Tip 💡: Remember the trio: Class is the state (Gas/Dust), Division is the situation (Everyday/Accident), and Group is the exact chemical (like Acetylene or Grain Dust)!
journeyman.explanation: Per 500.6, after Class and Division are established, the NEC uses 'Group' to further categorize the specific chemical or material. Class I gases are divided into Groups A through D; Class II dusts into Groups E, F, and G. Hydrogen (Group B) and gasoline (Group D) are distinguished by their Group letter.

journeyman.questionText: For a fire pump supply, how many disconnecting means and associated overcurrent protective devices are permitted before the fire pump controller?
options: As many as needed if grouped together
One for each fire pump motor

explanations.default: NEC 695.4(B)(1) permits only one disconnecting means and associated overcurrent protective device in the fire pump supply before the fire pump controller or transfer equipment.

journeyman.sparkyTip: Field Logic Tip 💡: Fire pumps are about reliability. Fewer shutdown points means less chance of accidentally killing power to the pump.
journeyman.explanation: Per 695.4(B)(1), only a single disconnecting means and associated overcurrent protective device are permitted in the supply path between the source and the listed fire pump controller. Multiple disconnects increase the chance of accidental power loss during a fire — the rule prioritizes reliability over selectivity.

journeyman.questionText: A fire pump is supplied through a disconnecting means and overcurrent protective device before reaching the listed fire pump controller. How many disconnecting means and associated overcurrent protective devices are permitted in this supply path?
options: As many as needed, if they are grouped together
No more than six disconnecting means
One disconnecting means and associated overcurrent protective device
One disconnecting means for each fire pump motor

explanations.default: NEC 695.4(B)(1) limits the fire pump supply connection to one disconnecting means and one associated overcurrent protective device between the power source and the listed fire pump controller, listed fire pump power transfer switch, or listed combination fire pump controller and power transfer switch. Fire pump circuits are treated differently because continuity of power is critical.

journeyman.sparkyTip: Field Logic Tip 💡: Fire pumps are not normal motor loads. The goal is to avoid unnecessary shutdown points. Think: one disconnect, one OCPD, keep the pump running.
journeyman.explanation: Per 695.4(B)(1), the fire pump supply may pass through exactly one disconnect and one OCPD before the listed controller. The intent is to minimize shutdown points. Multiple disconnects, grouping, or per-motor disconnects are not permitted in this supply path.

journeyman.questionText: How must a fire pump disconnecting means be marked?

explanations.default: NEC 695.4(B)(3)(c) requires the fire pump disconnecting means to be marked 'Fire Pump Disconnecting Means' in letters at least 1 inch high and visible without opening enclosure doors or covers.

journeyman.sparkyTip: Field Logic Tip 💡: Fire pump disconnects need to be obvious in an emergency. Look for red equipment and the exact marking: 'Fire Pump Disconnecting Means.'
journeyman.explanation: Per 695.4(B)(2)(a), the fire pump disconnecting means must be marked 'Fire Pump Disconnecting Means' with letters at least 1 inch high, and the marking must be visible without opening any doors or covers. Generic 'Emergency Disconnect' labeling does not satisfy this specific requirement.

journeyman.questionText: How high must energized fire pump equipment be located above the floor?

explanations.default: NEC 695.12(D) requires energized fire pump equipment to be located at least 12 inches above the floor level. This helps protect critical fire pump equipment from water that may be present in the pump room.

journeyman.sparkyTip: Field Logic Tip 💡: Fire pump equipment has to keep working during an emergency. Keep energized parts elevated—12 inches minimum above the floor.
journeyman.explanation: Per 695.12(E), energized fire pump equipment must be located at least 12 inches above the floor. This protects the equipment from minor flooding or water accumulation during a fire event when sprinklers are active.

journeyman.questionText: During a fire pump inspection, the disconnect is labeled “Emergency Disconnect” on the enclosure door. What is wrong with this installation?
options: The label must say “Fire Pump Disconnecting Means” and be visible without opening doors or covers
The label is acceptable if the disconnect is painted red
The label must say “Fire Pump Service Equipment” and be located inside the enclosure
The label is only required when more than one fire pump is installed

explanations.default: NEC 695.4(B)(3)(c) requires the fire pump disconnecting means to be marked “Fire Pump Disconnecting Means.” The marking must be in letters at least 1 inch high and visible without opening enclosure doors or covers. “Emergency Disconnect” is not the required marking.

journeyman.sparkyTip: Field Logic Tip 💡: Fire pump disconnect labels are not just general warning labels. The NEC wants specific wording that can be seen immediately: “Fire Pump Disconnecting Means.”
journeyman.explanation: Per 695.4(B)(2)(a), the fire pump disconnect must be labeled exactly 'Fire Pump Disconnecting Means' and the marking must be visible from outside without opening doors or covers. A generic 'Emergency Disconnect' label fails both the specific wording requirement and the external-visibility requirement.

journeyman.questionText: How must each outlet installed for electric vehicle charging be supplied?
options: By a general-purpose branch circuit shared with other receptacles
By a multiwire branch circuit only
By a feeder tap from the nearest panelboard
journeyman.explanation: NEC 625.40 requires each outlet installed for the purpose of charging electric vehicles to be supplied by an individual branch circuit.
journeyman.trapExplanation: The trap is thinking an EV charger outlet can be treated like a normal convenience receptacle. EV charging equipment has its own rule: the outlet for EV charging must be supplied by an individual branch circuit.
journeyman.sparkyTip: EV charging is a continuous load — give it its own circuit. Sharing with general-purpose receptacles is a violation, not just a bad idea.

journeyman.questionText: When installing a receptacle outlet for EV charging at a dwelling unit, the receptacle outlet must be identified for use with which equipment?
journeyman.explanation: NEC 625.40 requires each outlet installed for the purpose of EV charging to be supplied by an individual branch circuit. Where a receptacle outlet is installed for EV charging, it must be identified for use with EVSE or WPTE.
journeyman.trapExplanation: The trap is recognizing common electrical acronyms but choosing the one that matches Article 625 language. GFCI, AFCI, SPD, GFP, EMS, and UPS may appear in electrical work, but 625.40 is looking for EVSE or WPTE.
journeyman.sparkyTip: The receptacle itself must be EVSE-listed — not just a standard 14-50R installed near a charger. Look for the EVSE marking on the device.

journeyman.questionText: For an indoor EV charging installation, the coupler for EVSE must be located or stored not less than how high above the floor level?
journeyman.explanation: For indoor locations, NEC 625.50 requires the coupler for EVSE or WPTE to be located or stored not less than 18 inches above the floor level. For outdoor locations, the minimum is 24 inches above grade level.
journeyman.trapExplanation: The trap is missing the location. Indoor EV charging installations use 18 inches above the floor level, while outdoor installations use 24 inches above grade level.
journeyman.sparkyTip: Indoor: 18 inches minimum. Outdoor: 24 inches minimum. Memorize the pair — examiners love this one.

journeyman.questionText: You are laying out a new fire pump room. How must the fire pump controller and power transfer switches be positioned in relation to the mechanical pump itself?
options: They must be installed in an adjacent room with a 2-hour fire separation to avoid water exposure
They must be mounted in NEMA 4X enclosures regardless of their location in the pump room
They must be located or protected so they will not be damaged by water escaping from pumps or pump connections
They must be elevated at least 12 inches above the highest water level of the mechanical pump
journeyman.explanation: Fire pump rooms are inherently wet environments. NEC 695.12(E) explicitly mandates that controllers and power transfer switches must be located or physically protected so that escaping water from the pump or its connections won't cause damage. This is the specific code reference that backs up why drilling a hole in the top of a NEMA 2 drip-proof fire pump controller is a massive liability!
journeyman.sparkyTip: Always visualize the spray radius of a blown pump seal or a leaky packing gland when choosing the mounting location for your electrical equipment. (Also, don't confuse this with 695.12(D), which dictates that all energized parts must be at least 12 inches above the floor level!)

journeyman.questionText: When a single feeder supplies multiple elevator disconnects, what is required of the overcurrent protective devices (OCPDs) in each disconnect?
options: They must be selectively coordinated with supply-side OCPDs
They must feature a shunt-trip mechanism
They must be sized at 125% of the total load
journeyman.explanation: Under NEC 620.62, if multiple elevator disconnects share a single feeder, their OCPDs must be selectively coordinated with the supply side. This ensures a fault in one car doesn't trip the main feeder and take down the other elevators.
journeyman.sparkyTip: Selective coordination means the breaker closest to the fault trips first. For elevators sharing a feeder, it's mandatory, not optional.

master.questionText: A mobile home park has 2 lots, each with the minimum calculated load of 16,000 VA. Using Table 550.31 (55% demand factor for 2 lots), what is the total demand load for the park service?
master.explanation: Step 1: Total connected load = 2 lots × 16,000 VA = 32,000 VA. Step 2: Apply Table 550.31 demand factor for 2 lots = 55%. Step 3: 32,000 VA × 0.55 = 17,600 VA demand load.
master.sparkyTip: Always multiply first (lots × VA), then apply the demand factor. The demand factor accounts for diversity — not all homes peak at the same time.

master.questionText: Using a total demand load of 17,600 VA for a 2-lot mobile home park at 120/240V, what is the calculated service current?
master.explanation: For a single-phase 120/240V service: I = VA ÷ V = 17,600 VA ÷ 240V = 73.3 amperes. This is the calculated demand current used for sizing the park service conductors and overcurrent protection.
master.sparkyTip: Single-phase amps = VA ÷ 240V. Don't accidentally divide by 120V (that would double your answer) or use the three-phase formula.

master.questionText: An electrician bonds the neutral bus to the equipment grounding bus at the panelboard inside a new mobile home. Where should this bond be located instead?
options: At the first disconnect inside the mobile home
At any point along the feeder assembly
At the service disconnecting means outside the mobile home
Bonding is not required for mobile homes
master.explanation: Per NEC 550.16(A), the neutral and equipment grounding conductors must be kept separate in the mobile home. The neutral-to-ground bond must be made at the service disconnecting means (outdoor pedestal), not at the mobile home panelboard. The mobile home panel is a sub-panel fed from the service equipment outside.
master.sparkyTip: The mobile home panel is a sub-panel. The bond goes at the main disconnect outside — bonding inside would create parallel neutral current paths on the grounding conductor, which is a shock hazard.

master.questionText: A mobile home power-supply cord is measured at 38 feet from the attachment plug cap to the point of entrance. Which NEC requirement does this violate?
options: 550.10(A) — cord must be listed for 50 amperes
550.10(B) — cord must connect to the panelboard
master.explanation: Per NEC 550.10(D), the power-supply cord must not exceed 36½ feet (11.0 m) in length. At 38 feet, this cord exceeds the maximum by 1½ feet and must be replaced with a compliant cord.
master.sparkyTip: 550.10 has four parts: (A) = supply type, (B) = connection point, (C) = plug configuration, (D) = cord length. Master-level questions often ask you to cite the specific subsection!

master.questionText: A mobile home's calculated load per 550.18 is 13,500 VA at 120/240V. Can the standard 50-ampere power-supply cord serve this home?
options: Yes — the 50A cord can supply up to 15,000 VA
Yes — demand factors reduce the load below 50A
No — a permanently installed feeder is required
No — a 60A power-supply cord must be used instead
master.explanation: The current draw is 13,500 VA ÷ 240V = 56.25 amperes, which exceeds the 50-ampere cord rating. Per 550.10(A), when the calculated load exceeds the cord capacity, a permanently installed feeder rated not less than the calculated load must be used instead of a power-supply cord.
master.sparkyTip: A 50A cord at 240V maxes out at 12,000 VA (50 × 240). If the calculated load exceeds that, the Code requires a permanent feeder — there is no 60A cord option for mobile homes.

master.questionText: Per NEC 680.26(B)(2), the equipotential bonding grid must extend how far horizontally from the inside walls of a permanently installed pool?
master.explanation: NEC 680.26(B)(2) requires that the perimeter surface bonding extend for 3 feet horizontally beyond the inside walls of the pool. This ensures that anyone standing near the pool edge is on bonded surfaces, reducing the risk of shock from voltage differences.
master.sparkyTip: 3 feet out from the pool wall for the bonding grid. Think of it as creating a "safety zone" of equal potential around the entire pool perimeter.

master.questionText: Per NEC 680.44, what protection is required for all electrical equipment associated with a permanently installed indoor spa or hot tub, including the outlet supplying it?
options: GFCI protection for all 125V and 240V outlets
master.explanation: NEC 680.44 requires GFCI protection for all electrical equipment associated with a permanently installed indoor spa or hot tub. This includes the receptacle outlet supplying power, regardless of the voltage (125V or 240V), providing essential protection against electric shock in a wet environment.
master.sparkyTip: Indoor spas and hot tubs need GFCI on everything — even 240V circuits. This catches many people off guard since GFCI is often associated only with 120V receptacles.

master.questionText: Per NEC 680.51 and 680.52, what are the two key electrical safety requirements for a permanently installed fountain?
options: GFCI protection for all circuits and bonding of all metal parts
Only a disconnect switch within sight
Only double insulation on all conductors
master.explanation: NEC 680.51 requires GFCI protection for all electrical equipment associated with fountains, and NEC 680.52 requires bonding of all metal piping and metal parts of the fountain that are in contact with the water. Together these two requirements ensure both shock protection and equipotential bonding.
master.sparkyTip: Fountains get the same treatment as pools: GFCI everything + bond all metal. People wade in fountains too, so the NEC treats them seriously.

master.questionText: Per NEC 680.12, where must the disconnecting means for a pool motor be located?
options: Within sight from the pool motor and at least 5 feet from the inside wall of the pool
Anywhere within 50 feet of the pool
Adjacent to the pool pump with no minimum distance
master.explanation: NEC 680.12 requires that the disconnecting means for pool motors be within sight of the equipment it controls AND located at least 5 feet from the inside wall of the pool. "Within sight" means visible and not more than 50 feet away. This ensures a technician can see the equipment while operating the disconnect.
master.sparkyTip: Two rules for the pool motor disconnect: (1) within sight of the motor, and (2) at least 5 feet from the pool edge. Both must be satisfied — one without the other is a code violation.

master.questionText: What is the minimum clearance required for utility supply conductors (0-750V to ground, not insulated) above a pool?
master.explanation: NEC 680.8(A) requires that all conductors of any type (other than those specifically permitted in 680.8(B)) maintain a minimum 25-foot clearance over the maximum water level, diving structures, and observation areas. Insulated service drop cables get a reduced clearance of 22.5 feet, but bare or uninsulated utility conductors require the full 25 feet.
master.sparkyTip: 25 feet for bare/uninsulated utility lines over a pool, 22.5 feet for insulated service drops. The exam loves to test which clearance goes with which conductor type.

master.questionText: You are routing a single feeder to supply a bank of three elevators in a commercial high-rise. The feeder supplies a separate disconnecting means for each elevator driving machine. According to the NEC, what specific performance requirement must be met for the overcurrent protective devices (OCPDs) in these disconnects?
options: They must be electronically interlocked to prevent simultaneous starting of multiple driving machines
They must be selectively coordinated with any other supply-side overcurrent protective devices
They must utilize shunt-trip mechanisms tied to the primary fire alarm control panel
They must be sized at exactly 125 percent of the continuous load plus 250 percent of the noncontinuous load
master.explanation: Master exams love testing selective coordination! Under NEC 620.62, if multiple elevator disconnects are fed by a single feeder, their OCPDs must be selectively coordinated with the supply side. This ensures that a single fault in one elevator's circuit trips only its specific breaker, isolating the problem while keeping the other cars running.
master.sparkyTip: Selective coordination is a massive concept at the Master level. Remember: it ensures the breaker closest to the fault opens first. For multiple elevators on one feeder, it's mandatory, not just a design suggestion.
